abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns instantly to stone
Serpent Hair: Living, venomous snakes that writhe and strike independently
Flight: Golden wings enable swift aerial movement
Clairvoyance (implied ability to pose unanswerable riddles)
name: Greek Mythology
weaknesses: Mirrors: Cannot see her own reflection without petrifying herself
Indirect Vision: Can be fought by reflecting her gaze or using periphery
Mortal Body: Despite her powers, her physical form can be wounded
Emotional Vulnerability: Attachment can cloud her judgment
habitat: Solitary caves and temples, places of desolation
behavior: Victim turned protector, vengeful yet sympathetic, isolation-driven

christian.overview: The Sphinx as guardian asking riddles invites comparison with biblical cherubim and Wisdom calling at the gates.

biblicalParallels.description: Living creatures with human and lion faces echo the Sphinx's hybrid form.
Cherubim guarding Eden parallel the Sphinx's threshold role.
Wisdom calling at the gates parallels the Sphinx's riddle.

patristicInterpretation.summary: The Sphinx's riddle about human nature finds its answer in Christ.

medievalSymbolism.bestiaryMeaning: Union of strength (lion) with wisdom (human) properly fulfilled in Christ.

christian.typologicalReading: The Sphinx's riddle finds ultimate answer in Christian anthropology.

keyMyths.title: Oedipus and the Riddle
keyMyths.description: The Sphinx terrorized Thebes, devouring anyone who could not answer her riddle: "What walks on four legs in the morning, two in the afternoon, and three in the evening?" Oedipus correctly answered "Man," causing the Sphinx to throw herself from her high rock to her death.

keyMyths.title: The Sphinx's Origins
keyMyths.description: The Sphinx is often described as a child of Typhon and Echidna, monstrous offspring of Gaia. This parentage aligns her with other terrifying creatures of Greek myth and explains her fearsome nature.

keyMyths.title: Hera's Envoy of Punishment
keyMyths.description: Some accounts claim Hera sent the Sphinx to punish the city of Thebes. This highlights the Sphinx's role as an agent of divine retribution, enforcing the will of the gods.

keyMyths.title: The Guard of Thebes
keyMyths.description: The Sphinx was stationed outside Thebes, guarding the city's entrance. She challenged all who sought passage, highlighting her role as a protective, albeit deadly, guardian.

keyMyths.title: Variations in Appearance
keyMyths.description: While typically depicted with the body of a lion, wings of an eagle, and the face of a woman, some accounts vary in the Sphinx's appearance.  These variations demonstrate the fluid nature of mythical creatures across different tellings.

sources.name: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex
sources.description: A classical Athenian tragedy that recounts the myth of Oedipus and his encounter with the Sphinx.
sources.title: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex

sources.name: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.description: A major source for Greek mythology, particularly the origins of the gods and monsters.
sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony

sources.name: Pseudo-Apollodorus, Bibliotheca
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Greek myths and heroic legends.
sources.title: Pseudo-Apollodorus, Bibliotheca

sources.name: Pausanias, Description of Greece
sources.description: A second-century AD travelogue that provides valuable information about Greek mythology and ancient sites.
sources.title: Pausanias, Description of Greece

sources.name: Hyginus, Fabulae
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories and summaries, providing insights into various Greek myths.
sources.title: Hyginus, Fabulae

sources.name: Tripp, Edward. *Crowell's Handbook of Classical Mythology.*
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to Greek and Roman mythology, providing detailed information about various myths and characters.
sources.title: Tripp, Edward. *Crowell's Handbook of Classical Mythology.*
